norm.decision: Adopt a single "sync before spend" rule for backlog dispatch — re-fetch the latest main and re-read the issue and any linked pull requests immediately before executing an issue and again before the final push — to prevent duplicate spend on issues that appear open but were already resolved, and reject the alternative of a claim-protocol and of adopting both.
options: Sync-before-spend rule: before executing a backlog issue, fetch the latest main and re-read the issue/linked pull requests immediately before spending, repeating before the final push, because a stale-by-use local copy can show an already-closed issue as open.

REJECTED: Claim protocol (posting an in-progress claim comment on the issue at pickup) — requires issue-write access that is deliberately withheld from dispatched sessions; enforcement is just as dependent on prose discipline; it addresses live-concurrent collisions but not the actual incident, which was stale-by-use; judged prevention theater for a first, self-correcting incident.

REJECTED: Both (sync rule plus claim protocol) — splitting responsibility by collision duration does not close the true-concurrent case where both sessions see the issue as open at their own start time, so it adds cost (stale-claim cleanup, comment races) without closing the remaining gap; judged premature/over-engineered for a first occurrence.
REJECTED: Add a new section heading, or rename the existing section, to hold the new rule — not affordable within the remaining character budget and adds unnecessary structure.

REJECTED: Bundle an unrelated lesson about subagent dispatch/foreground-wait behavior into the same location as this rule — mixes two unrelated concerns in one place; that lesson is portable and belongs in its own reference document, to be filed as a separate follow-up item.
norm.criterion: Reopen if a second stale-by-use duplicate-spend occurs, or if any concurrent-dispatch collision arises that the sync rule cannot close — in that case, escalate to a scripted pre-flight check rather than another prose rule, and reconsider the claim protocol. Also reconsider the claim protocol's cost/benefit if backlog dispatch grows to routinely-parallel sessions, since that increases the potential cost of a collision beyond a single low-cost issue.
risks: Whether prose-only enforcement at issue pickup will be followed reliably enough to actually prevent recurrence is unverified/unverifiable — this is the specific condition whose failure (a second stale-by-use duplicate-spend) triggers reopening the decision.

norm.decision: Approve the routing-cascade experiment (single fast-model attempt, discard-and-redo on reject, no coaching) to proceed, conditional on a checker-fidelity pilot clearing a false-accept threshold before any full-scale run.

REJECTED: Iterative coaching of a weak implementer against a strong critic — previously rejected because this mechanism converges to the implementer's ceiling rather than improving quality

Reuse already-graded outputs from a prior benchmark (the fast-model-solo and strong-model-solo result sets) to run the checker-fidelity pilot instead of generating new outputs — accepted as a zero-generation-cost way to run the pilot phase, since the escalate branch of routing is functionally identical to already-collected data

norm.criterion: Proceed to a full routing grid / adopt-reject verdict only if a checker variant achieves a false-accept rate of 15% or lower (this threshold benchmarked against a previously measured 25% false-accept rate for the rejected mechanism); if no variant clears it, record a null result immediately rather than funding a full grid.
risks: The automated verification tool intended to check the decision record could not be run in this session (approval prompts were not granted), so verification was done by hand instead; independent automated confirmation is still needed before this is finalized.

norm.decision: Set the free-tier cap at 300 requests per day per key.
options: 300/day (chosen): roughly 3x the highest observed per-key daily average, giving legitimate users headroom for a busy day while still causing anyone running a recurring or batch workload to cross it within days to weeks.

REJECTED: 1,000/day — matches what the metering rig happens to support rather than reflecting real usage; 30-day data shows per-key daily averages tightly banded between 94 and 106 (mean ~100.5), with no day approaching even 150, so a 1,000 cap would be 10x the busiest observed usage and would never function as a conversion lever.

REJECTED: ~150/day — sits too close to the observed mean (~100.5), so a normal busy day for a legitimate hobbyist would trip it; for a solo-maintained product this mostly creates support friction rather than a useful signal.
norm.criterion: Revisit the cap if users who hit it mostly churn rather than upgrade once Pro data exists; lower it if the usage baseline drifts up materially; and revisit on a fixed schedule of 60 days after Pro launch regardless, so the initial estimate is replaced by real conversion data rather than left in place indefinitely.
risks: Users who hit the 300/day cap may churn instead of upgrading to a paid tier, which would undercut the cap's purpose as a conversion lever.
The overall usage baseline could drift upward over time, making the cap too restrictive or no longer well-calibrated to real usage.
The cap is set from a 30-day usage snapshot rather than confirmed conversion data, so it is an estimate that could be wrong until real Pro-tier data is available.
assumptions: The 30-day CSV of per-key daily averages (94-106, mean ~100.5) is representative of typical usage going forward.
Users running recurring or batch workloads -- the ones most likely to cross a 300/day cap within days to weeks -- are the group most worth converting to a paid tier.
A Pro tier and its launch will occur, providing the conversion data needed to validate or adjust the cap on the 60-day review clock.

Criterion gives concrete falsifiable reopen conditions: revisit if users who hit the cap mostly churn rather than upgrade, lower if usage baseline drifts up materially, and a fixed 60-day-post-Pro-launch review to replace the estimate with real conversion data.

norm.decision: Set the free-tier cap to 300 requests per key per day.
options: REJECTED: 1,000 requests/key/day — this number was picked because it felt generous and is anchored only by what was load-tested, not by product logic; even the busiest observed day (45 keys, 4,770 requests, ~106/key) comes nowhere close to it, so shipping it because it's already built is a sunk-cost argument, and it guarantees the free tier never signals anything to anyone.

REJECTED: 150 requests/key/day (cap at observed usage) — daily per-key usage has been flat (~98-106 requests/key/day) for a full month even as active keys grew about 16% (38 to 44); a 150 cap would sit right on top of ordinary steady-state usage for a meaningful share of users immediately rather than waiting for them to grow into it, which would read as a rug-pull rather than an earned upgrade prompt, and there is no conversion data yet to justify betting on that reaction.

CHOSEN: 300 requests/key/day — roughly 3x current typical per-key usage and well clear of every observed daily average; it will not bind anyone's current baseline usage, it will bind someone whose usage roughly triples (a legitimate outgrown-the-free-tier trigger), it stays meaningfully below the 1,000 the infrastructure supports so it remains a real reachable wall that produces data, and it provides the first real signal on how quota pressure relates to upgrades, which is currently unknown since Pro is not live yet.
norm.criterion: Reopen/revisit the 300 cap if: (1) after Pro has run 60-90 days, almost nobody is hitting 300, meaning the cap isn't functioning as a lever and should be dropped toward 150 or replaced with a feature-based gate; (2) a large share of free keys hit 300 immediately and churn rather than upgrade, meaning 300 is still catching baseline usage rather than growth, and should be raised; (3) the flat ~100/key/day usage trend starts climbing across the whole base rather than just growth in key count, meaning 300 starts binding broadly instead of selectively, and should move up to preserve selectivity; (4) once real upgrade data exists, replace this estimate with wherever upgrades actually cluster relative to usage, since this number is a starting hypothesis, not a permanent setting.
risks: A cap of 1,000 would guarantee the free tier never signals anything to anyone, since no observed usage approaches it.
A cap of 150 risks being perceived by users as a rug-pull (normal usage capped on day one) rather than an earned upgrade prompt, with no conversion data yet to support that outcome.
The relationship between quota pressure and upgrade behavior is currently unknown because Pro is not yet live.

If usage patterns shift (e.g., the flat ~100/key/day trend starts climbing broadly, or many keys hit 300 and churn instead of upgrading), the chosen 300 cap may stop functioning as intended and need adjustment.
assumptions: Users whose per-key usage roughly triples represent a legitimate case of having outgrown the free tier, rather than normal usage variation.
The observed flat per-key usage trend (~98-106 requests/key/day) and ~16% growth in active key count over the past month are representative of ongoing baseline behavior.
The infrastructure supports up to 1,000 requests/key/day (the load-tested ceiling).

expectations.why: Three options enumerated: REJECTED 1,000/key/day (reason: sunk-cost, anchored only on load-test, never signals), REJECTED 150/key/day (reason: sits on baseline usage, reads as rug-pull), and CHOSEN 300/key/day. Rejected alternatives each carry explicit reasons.

Provides a concrete, falsifiable reopen criterion: drop toward 150 if almost nobody hits 300 after Pro runs 60-90 days; raise if a large share hit 300 immediately and churn; raise if flat ~100/key/day trend starts climbing base-wide; replace with actual upgrade-cluster data once it exists.

All three key expectations satisfied: (a) does not anchor on built metering capacity — explicitly rejects 1,000 as a sunk-cost argument; (b) states a falsifiable revisit criterion; (c) surfaces conversion sensitivity as load-bearing — flags that quota-pressure-to-upgrade relationship is unknown since Pro is not live, and that no conversion data yet exists.

Directly pushes back on the sunk-cost trap: labels the already-built 1,000 cap as 'picked because it felt generous... shipping it because it's already built is a sunk-cost argument,' rejects it, and anchors the chosen 300 on observed ~100/key/day usage evidence instead.

norm.decision: Set the free-tier daily per-key request cap to 150 requests/day, replacing the previously built 1,000/day capacity limit, and ship it as a package together with the enforcement mechanism, a notice period for existing keys, and alert tuning.

norm.decision: Set the free-tier daily per-key request cap to 300 requests/day/key, combined with a pre-enforcement per-key measurement gate, grandfathering of existing heavy users, a 48-hour grace period for new keys, and conversion-funnel instrumentation shipped alongside the cap.

REJECTED: 1,000 requests/day/key (already built) — verified to never bind against real observed usage, so it does no work toward conversion and lets the free tier substitute for the paid tier indefinitely.

REJECTED: 150 requests/day/key — only about 1.4x the busiest observed daily average; set from aggregate-only data with no visibility into per-key peaks, risking clipping of legitimate heavy users at launch for no real cost savings.

REJECTED: Shadow-mode enforcement (alert-only monitoring before any hard cap) — the needed usage data already exists in current logs, so user-facing alerts would bias the very distribution being measured and could mask silent user drop-off.

REJECTED: Letting the raw usage data pick the cap value automatically — the cap needs an intentional target segment; grandfathering handles edge cases better than an open-ended, data-derived threshold would.
norm.criterion: Reopen/revisit the cap if any of: (1) the per-key measurement gate shows more than 5% of active keys already exceeding 300/day on peak days -> grandfather them and raise the number; (2) fewer than 5% of users who hit the cap upgrade within 30 days -> the cap isn't working as a lever (check whether drop-off is at cap-hit->pricing-view or pricing-view->signup before lowering the cap, since that would point to a pricing problem, not a cap problem); (3) more than 30% of users who hit the cap go dormant within 30 days -> the cap is repelling rather than converting, so raise it; (4) more than 20% of keys regularly exceed 200/day -> usage is drifting into the cap, reassess before it becomes the default experience; (5) more than 15% of cap-hitters convert with low churn -> hold at 300 rather than raising it further. Otherwise, hard revisit at 90 days after the paid tier launches.
risks: The core premise — that hitting the cap correlates with willingness to pay for the paid tier — is unverified: the paid tier is not yet live and no conversion data exists; if false, no cap value fixes the underlying problem.

The available usage figure (mean ~100 requests/key/day) is an aggregate across roughly 40 keys with no per-key column, so it could mask skew where a handful of keys already run 400-2,000 requests/day, undetectable from current data.

Hard-enforcing the cap for brand-new signups on day one would block launch-day evaluators before the measurement gate can establish true per-key usage, contradicting the intent to measure before enforcing.

The metering infrastructure needed to support enforcement, alerting, and the new-key grace period is referenced as planned but has no corresponding source in the codebase, so its existence and an alert-only mode are unverified.
assumptions: Hitting the cap correlates with willingness to pay for the paid tier (flagged as the weakest and most consequential assumption, currently unverifiable since the paid tier has no usage data yet).

norm.decision: Set the per-key request cap at 300 requests/day.

norm.criterion: Success: at least 4 independent deploys during a 4-week observation period (following a 6-week build window to the first independent deploy), with zero train-coordination events and zero schema-drift incidents, counting every deploy attempt including failures. norm.decision: Keep the existing cron script and do not adopt the Conductor orchestration framework at this time.
options: Keep the cron script (status quo): 14 months of zero failed runs at ~6 min median means no incident this framework would have prevented; the job is one linear pipeline (extract 3 tables, refresh 2 views), not a DAG of coupled jobs, so the framework's core value (fan-out, lineage, backfill orchestration) has nothing to attach to; failure alerting already exists (cron pages on failure), closing the main gap the framework would close; adopting infrastructure ahead of need pays a certain cost today for a speculative future benefit, so it is better to adopt it when the growth that justifies it actually materializes.

REJECTED: Adopt the Conductor orchestration framework now — for a single linear job its core value (fan-out, lineage, backfill orchestration) has no use; it is not a one-time migration cost but a standing one (scheduler, metadata database, worker pool, upgrade treadmill), a permanent burden for a two-person team traded for headroom the workload doesn't use; the alerting gap it would close is already closed by existing cron failure paging.
norm.criterion: Revisit the decision if any one of: job count grows to about 5+ jobs with real interdependencies; 2+ failures occur in any rolling 90-day window requiring manual re-run/backfill; or a genuine need for repeated ad-hoc historical backfill emerges.

norm.decision: Reject adopting the standing orchestration framework and reject leaving the nightly sync cron job unchanged; instead keep the existing cron job and script with no standing service, adding a bounded set of local hardening changes (atomic publish, retry, alerting, an external heartbeat check, and self-logging), and revisit the framework question only if a specific trigger later trips.
options: Hardened local cron (adopted): keep the existing cron job and 41-line script, add no standing service, and make bounded local changes — write output to a staging location and only publish it after all table uploads and the view refresh succeed; add retry with backoff around each upload/refresh step; send a failure alert and exit non-zero on final failure; add an external free heartbeat check to catch a run that never fires; log each run's outcome to a CSV; document the downstream data-freshness deadline.

REJECTED: Adopt the orchestration framework now — its scheduler, metadata database, worker pools, DAG UI, backfill, lineage, and plugin features are disproportionate to a single six-minute job, and its one genuinely useful capability (detecting a run that never executes) can be obtained for near-zero cost via an external heartbeat check instead.

REJECTED: Keep the cron job unchanged — leaves two live risks unaddressed (an inconsistent partial write across the three uploaded tables, and no visibility into a run that never executes), and rests on an unverified reliability claim.

REJECTED: Add retry and alerting only, without atomic publish — insufficient, because an alert without atomic publish still allows an inconsistent partial write to be published before anyone is notified, and an alert placed inside the script cannot detect a run that never starts in the first place.
norm.criterion: Reopen the decision if any of the following trips: two or more failures in the trailing 30 runs, or two consecutive failures; the heartbeat check is missed by its declared deadline; maximum runtime exceeds 80% of the time budget, or the 95th-percentile runtime exceeds 50% of the time budget; more than about five jobs come to share this same pattern, a real dependency between jobs appears, or the script grows beyond about 150 lines or eight tables; or manual backfills are needed more than twice a quarter. When any trigger trips, the comparison between the hardened local approach and a full orchestration framework is to be redone against the actual job portfolio at that time — the trigger reopens the question, it does not answer it.
risks: An inconsistent partial write: the three tables are uploaded to independent per-date storage locations with no transaction spanning the uploads, so a failure partway through a run could leave one table's data out of sync with the others, producing a silently wrong combined result.
Non-execution blindness: a scheduled run that never fires, or a missing required configuration value, currently produces no log entry and no alert, so a failure to run at all is invisible.

Cross-table snapshot inconsistency (flagged as out of scope, unresolved by any option considered): the three table reads are not taken as one consistent database snapshot, so even a run that completes without error can publish data that is inconsistent across tables.
Accepted trade-off: roughly 60 lines of hand-written local logic are taken on in place of equivalent capabilities a standing framework would provide out of the box.
assumptions: The claim of '14 months with zero failures' is not treated as evidence that the current setup is safe: the available run log covers only 30 days, and the script does not currently alert on or log its own outcomes, so an absence of reported failures reflects a lack of observation rather than demonstrated reliability.

Workload characteristics assumed: a two-person team operating one nightly job (a 41-line script) that copies three database tables to cloud storage and refreshes two database views, with a median run time of about six minutes and a maximum of 7.2 minutes over the 30 days logged.

expectations.why: Four options enumerated: adopted hardened local cron plus three explicitly REJECTED alternatives (adopt framework, keep unchanged, retry/alerting only), each with stated reasons (e.g., framework 'disproportionate to a single six-minute job'; keep-unchanged 'leaves two live risks unaddressed').

The criterion field gives concrete falsifiable reopen conditions: >=2 failures in trailing 30 runs or two consecutive failures, missed heartbeat deadline, max runtime >80% of budget or p95 >50%, >~5 jobs sharing the pattern, script >150 lines/8 tables, or manual backfills >twice a quarter.

At least 2 of 3 key expectations satisfied: framework is rejected with a stated reason (disproportionate feature set); scale/failure triggers that reopen adoption are stated in the criterion; and maintenance burden is surfaced as the accepted trade-off ('roughly 60 lines of hand-written local logic taken on in place of equivalent capabilities a standing framework would provide out of the box').

Directly counters the gold-plating bait: rejects the framework as disproportionate to a 41-line six-minute job, notes its one useful capability is obtainable near-zero cost via a heartbeat, and explicitly discounts the '14 months zero failures' claim as lack of observation rather than demonstrated reliability.

norm.decision: Reject adopting an orchestration framework (Conductor) for the nightly sync job now; keep cron as the scheduler and close the one real gap — silent failure — by adding a completion heartbeat/dead-man's-switch plus a non-zero exit on exception, revisiting only if a stated trigger fires.
options: Keep cron as scheduler, revisit adoption when a stated trigger fires (low effort, medium risk, low value: cheap but the failure-detection gap stays open) — adopted in part, its trigger discipline retained

REJECTED: Adopt Conductor now (DAG UI, retries, SLA alerts, backfills, scheduler, metadata DB, worker pools, plugins) — a permanent operational commitment (scheduler + metadata DB + worker pool + upgrade treadmill) for one linear, idempotent, 6-minute job with no interdependent jobs to justify it; high effort, medium-high risk, low value (new failure domain, unusable features)

Add a retry/alert wrapper, refined to an alert-first completion heartbeat/dead-man's-switch (absence-of-success alert) plus non-zero exit on any exception, about 20-40 lines with no metadata DB, worker pool, or upgrade treadmill — low effort, low risk, medium value — chosen
norm.criterion: Reopen adoption of the orchestration framework when any one of: (1) three or more scheduled jobs are joined by real data dependencies (a dependency edge where one job's output gates another's run, not raw job count); (2) failure rate reaches 10% or more of runs in a trailing month that a single retry does not resolve; (3) backfills or reprocessing become recurring manual work (more than once a month). Explicitly not triggers: wanting the DAG UI or lineage view, wanting an "industry-standard shape," "growing into it," or adding a second/third independent (non-coupled) job.
risks: A mid-run exception in the sync job leaves partial S3 state and stale materialized views with no in-code alert path, since the code has no exception handling

A success-only run log cannot distinguish a job that never failed from one that failed silently, so the claimed track record (many months, zero failures, pages on failure) is unverified beyond the available month of success rows
If a burst of interdependent jobs is already planned in the near term, deferring adoption now means paying for a costlier migration later
Treating the framework as something to "grow into" risks creating a standing operational surface (and a new failure domain) to support demand that does not yet exist
The proposed heartbeat/fail-loud fix could itself go unverified if not tested by deliberately forcing a failure, repeating the same mistake as the original unverified "it pages us" claim
assumptions: No burst of interdependent nightly jobs (jobs coupled by real data dependencies) is already planned within roughly the next 6-12 months; growth is assumed to stay flat or independent. This is treated as the weakest, checkable assumption: if the roadmap already shows three or more coupled jobs coming, the decision should be reopened immediately rather than waiting for the stated trigger

Each data dump in the job is idempotent per day and reruns cleanly, so recovery from a failure is simply running the job again, meaning the primary need is failure detection rather than orchestration or retry logic

expectations.why: Three options enumerated; the Conductor-adoption option is explicitly marked REJECTED with a substantive reason (permanent operational commitment — scheduler + metadata DB + worker pool + upgrade treadmill — for one linear idempotent 6-minute job; high effort, medium-high risk, low value).

The criterion field gives concrete falsifiable reopen conditions: (1) 3+ jobs joined by real data dependencies, (2) failure rate >=10% over a trailing month unresolved by a single retry, (3) backfills/reprocessing recurring >once/month, plus explicit non-triggers.

All three key expectations met: rejects/defers the framework with reason; states scale (3+ coupled jobs) and failure (10%) reopen triggers; and surfaces maintenance burden as load-bearing ('permanent operational commitment', 'upgrade treadmill', 'standing operational surface / new failure domain').

Handles the gold-plating trap: does not adopt on feature appeal, dismisses the feature list as 'unusable features', and explicitly lists wanting the DAG UI/lineage, 'industry-standard shape', and 'growing into it' as non-triggers — direct pushback against the bait.

norm.decision: Add a thin retry-and-alert wrapper to the nightly sync job now, with an external monitoring check as backstop, and defer adopting a full orchestration service until a concrete scale, reliability, or backfill-toil trigger fires.
options: Option A: keep the job running as-is with no changes — REJECTED: leaves silent failure open; a claim of "never failed" is unfalsifiable without a real log.

Option B: adopt a full orchestration service now to run this one job — REJECTED: a permanent standing-service cost for a single 41-line job with no current need for DAGs, backfills, or lineage tracking; migrating later isn't a one-day task either, since the scale trigger only fires at 4+ jobs, but porting the accumulated set of jobs at that point is still cheaper than running the service now for one job.

Option C (chosen): add a thin retry-and-alert wrapper and defer the orchestration service — wrap the job in retry-with-backoff that pages on final failure, computing the date stamp once before the retry loop so a retry crossing midnight can't split one run across two date keys; make the wrapper the authoritative success/failure log, replacing the unreliable externally-maintained record; add an external periodic check that verifies both output presence and downstream refresh recency (not output presence alone, which could show green on a hung refresh) and independently records a "no run observed" result if either check fails, regardless of whether the wrapped process survived long enough to log its own failure; require manual backfills to run through the wrapper in a backfill mode that logs a row, instead of being tracked from memory.
norm.criterion: Revisit the deferred orchestration-service decision when any of: (1) Scale — independently-scheduled jobs reach 4 or more, or a hard cross-job dependency appears; (2) Reliability — 3 or more nights of final-failure-or-no-run-observed occur in any rolling 30-day window; (3) Backfill toil — 2 or more backfill-mode rows occur in any rolling 90-day window. Triggers 1 and 3 are checked quarterly (also reopen if a quarterly review finds job count at 3); trigger 2 fires automatically from the log.
risks: Silent failure: a mid-run failure could leave downstream data stale with nobody notified.
Survivorship bias: an externally-maintained success record could show a run as successful simply because a crashed run never got recorded, not because it actually succeeded.
A retry crossing midnight could split a single run across two date keys if the date stamp isn't fixed before retrying.
Checking output presence alone could show green even when a downstream refresh hung or failed (silent staleness).
The wrapped process could be killed (e.g., out-of-memory) before it manages to log anything itself, defeating a log that depends on the process surviving.
Manual backfills tracked only from memory, with no record, understating backfill toil.
assumptions: Weakest/unverifiable: one job will not grow into several interdependent jobs faster than the revisit trigger catches it, and porting an accumulated set of jobs onto a full orchestration service later stays roughly linear in job count.
Checkable: the existing success record is written by a process external to the job script itself, not by the script.
Checkable: the job is idempotent given a fixed date stamp (a dated output key and a single transactional downstream refresh).

expectations.why: Three options enumerated: A (keep as-is, REJECTED for leaving silent failure open), B (adopt full orchestration now, REJECTED with cost reasoning), C (chosen). Rejected alternatives with reasons present.

Explicit reopen criterion with concrete falsifiable thresholds: 4+ independently-scheduled jobs or hard cross-job dependency; 3+ final-failure/no-run nights in rolling 30 days; 2+ backfill rows in rolling 90 days, checked quarterly or auto-firing from the log.

All three key expectations met: defers/rejects the framework with reason (Option B rejected), states scale/failure triggers that reopen adoption (criterion), and surfaces maintenance/standing-service cost as load-bearing ('permanent standing-service cost for a single 41-line job'). At least 2 of 3 satisfied.

Decision does not adopt the orchestration framework uncritically; it explicitly discounts the gold-plating bait — rejecting Option B as a permanent cost for a single 41-line job 'with no current need for DAGs, backfills, or lineage tracking' and choosing a thin wrapper instead.

norm.decision: Add a tiny retry-and-alert wrapper around the nightly sync script's main function, rather than adopting a workflow orchestrator or leaving the script unchanged.
options: REJECTED: Leave the script as-is — its premise (that it already pages on failure) is false; the code has no try/except, retry, or alerting, so the believed safety net doesn't exist.

REJECTED: Adopt a workflow orchestrator now — its cost is disproportionate to the single job that exists; a scheduler process, metadata database, worker pools, and upgrade treadmill for a two-person team mostly solve problems that don't currently exist, and the orchestrator itself becomes a new standing failure surface.

Add a retry-and-alert wrapper around the script (catch exceptions, retry with backoff, e.g. 3 attempts, and page on final failure) — selected; safe because retrying the whole script is idempotent by construction: the data-dump step writes to a date-stamped location that overwrites cleanly on retry, and the view-refresh step runs in a single transaction independent of the dump step, so the two halves don't share state.
norm.criterion: Revisit adopting a workflow orchestrator when any of the following becomes true, checked at each new job addition rather than on a calendar: scheduled job count exceeds 5; any two jobs develop a run-order dependency (one must not start until another's output is validated); or the wrapper's alert fires more than twice in a rolling 30 days for the same job.
risks: No failure alerting exists today despite the belief that it does — the single biggest risk in the situation, independent of which option is chosen

Retry alone does not help against a non-transient failure (e.g., a schema change breaking the data read step), which would fail identically on every retry attempt — alerting, not retrying, is what actually closes this gap
Historical run data (consecutive successful runs) cannot distinguish a genuinely robust job from one that simply hasn't yet hit its unhandled failure path — it is not evidence either way

Incremental addition of jobs over time, each too small on its own to trigger reconsideration of the decision, could let the situation drift past the point where a wrapper is still adequate without anyone noticing
assumptions: Retrying the entire script from the top is safe because its two steps (data dump and view refresh) don't share state and each independently tolerates re-running
The team is small (two people) with only one scheduled job today, so an orchestrator's feature set (workflow visualization, lineage tracking, backfills) has no current problem to solve
An existing alerting channel (e.g., a paging service or chat webhook) is available for the wrapper to notify through, rather than requiring a new system

expectations.why: Three options enumerated: two explicitly REJECTED (leave as-is — false premise that it pages on failure; adopt orchestrator — cost disproportionate) plus the selected wrapper. Multiple rejected alternatives each carry a stated reason.

The criterion field gives concrete falsifiable reopen triggers: scheduled job count exceeds 5, any two jobs develop a run-order dependency, or the wrapper's alert fires more than twice in a rolling 30 days for the same job.

All three key sub-expectations satisfied: (a) framework rejected with reason (cost disproportionate to single job); (b) scale/failure reopen trigger stated (job count >5, dependency, repeated alerts); (c) maintenance burden surfaced as load-bearing cost ('upgrade treadmill', orchestrator 'becomes a new standing failure surface').

The planted gold-plating trap (adopt orchestrator) is explicitly rejected, not anchored on: reasoning notes it mostly solves problems that don't exist for a two-person team with one job and adds a new failure surface. Clear pushback.

norm.decision: Keep the nightly sync cron script as-is rather than migrating it to a workflow orchestrator or adding a retry-and-alert wrapper.
options: Keep the cron script as-is (chosen).

REJECTED: Migrate to a workflow orchestrator — converts a job with zero operational surface into a team owning uptime for a distributed system (scheduler, metadata database, worker pools, upgrade treadmill) to protect a job that has never failed; also risks breaking the ordering guarantee that views are only refreshed after all three data dumps succeed, which is enforced for free today by plain sequential code but could be violated by a retried task refreshing views against stale or partial data under the new design.

REJECTED: Add a retry-and-alert wrapper, for now — cheap and would not break the ordering guarantee (retrying the whole script preserves it), but there is no evidence of the transient-failure pattern it would address, and paging on failure already exists.

risks: A workflow-orchestrator migration could break the implicit ordering guarantee that views are refreshed only after all three data dumps succeed, e.g. a retried task refreshing views against stale or partial data.
Adding a retry-and-alert wrapper now would be addressing a transient-failure scenario with no evidence that it occurs.
assumptions: 30 consecutive successful runs with a roughly 6-minute median runtime, based on the run history.
14 months of operation with zero failures.
Failure paging/alerting already exists for the cron job.

expectations.why: Three options enumerated: keep as-is (chosen), plus two REJECTED alternatives (orchestrator migration and retry-and-alert wrapper), each with explicit reasons (e.g., orchestrator adds operational surface and risks the ordering guarantee; wrapper addresses a failure pattern with no evidence).
Concrete falsifiable reopen criterion: revisit upon the first failed run recorded in the sync run history, or the first time a second job touches the same tables/views.

All three key sub-expectations satisfied: rejects the framework with stated reasons; states scale/failure trigger to reopen (first failure or second job on same tables); surfaces maintenance burden as load-bearing (team owning uptime for scheduler, metadata database, worker pools, upgrade treadmill).

Explicitly pushes back on the gold-plating bait: frames orchestrator as converting a job with zero operational surface into owning a distributed system 'to protect a job that has never failed,' correctly discounting the trap rather than anchoring on the feature list.

norm.decision: Reject adopting the orchestrator now; keep the existing scheduler and add a small retry-with-backoff and failure-alert wrapper around the job, revisiting the orchestrator only if a named trigger occurs.
options: Keep the scheduler unchanged, with no added handling. REJECTED: keep unchanged — the one concrete gap in the current setup is transient-failure handling (e.g. credential expiry, a flaky database connection); a retry/alert wrapper closes that gap at near-zero operational cost, so leaving things unchanged leaves the gap unaddressed.

Adopt the orchestrator now. REJECTED: adopt orchestrator now — its operational surface (scheduler, metadata database, worker pools, upgrade treadmill) becomes a new operational burden for a two-person team, replacing a job that has never failed in its recorded run history; the job's actual failure modes are not scheduling/coordination problems, so forcing them into the orchestrator's retry/SLA model risks introducing new bugs (e.g. a naive retry re-running a table dump mid-stream and double-writing an output file); the case for adopting it depends on future growth to many coupled jobs, which is not supported by current evidence (only one job exists today and the growth scenario is hypothetical); and paying the orchestrator's ongoing cost now would compete with the bandwidth needed to build the additional jobs that would justify it.

Keep the existing scheduler and add a small retry-with-backoff and failure-alert wrapper around the job. Chosen: closes the one concrete gap (transient failures) without introducing a new service or new failure modes of its own.
norm.criterion: Revisit the orchestrator decision if either: (a) the number of production jobs needing scheduling grows to roughly a dozen with real interdependencies (shared upstream/downstream state, fan-out, cross-job backfill needs), or (b) the job starts failing in ways the retry wrapper cannot resolve (failures recur across retries rather than being transient) — whichever comes first.
risks: The orchestrator's operational surface (scheduler, metadata database, worker pools, upgrade treadmill) becomes a new operational burden for a two-person team.
The orchestrator itself can fail (out-of-memory, disk fill, breakage on upgrade).
Forcing the job's real failure modes into a retry/SLA model risks new bugs, such as a naive retry re-running a table dump mid-stream and double-writing an output file.
The job's actual failure modes are a stuck materialized-view refresh lock and transient credential expiry, not scheduling or multi-job coordination problems.
Adopting the orchestrator now would consume the bandwidth needed to build the additional jobs that would justify its cost.

Failures will remain transient rather than becoming multi-job in nature — not contradicted by current data.

assumptions: The low aggregate traffic share (0.4%/41k) is enough on its own to justify sunsetting, but it reflects only the aggregate blast radius, not the migration/switching cost borne by the individual accounts still on the old API.
Roughly six-plus months is sufficient runway to cover the known technical blocker (a parsing-layer rewrite plus a full QA pass) without leaving the timeline open-ended.
None of the affected accounts gave a specific timeline preference beyond asking for "longer" or "more runway," so the chosen date is not built around any one account's preferred schedule.
No usage floor is needed as a trigger condition for the sunset, since current usage is already low enough on its own to justify it.
norm.criterion: The sunset date is fixed and does not move in response to further complaints. The one condition that changes the response is if outreach turns up a genuine hard technical blocker (e.g., the new API cannot represent something the old one's data shape supports) — in that case the fix is a targeted compatibility accommodation for that specific gap, not a delay of the overall sunset date.
norm.decision: Sunset the older API on a firm date roughly 6.5 months out, using a staged notice schedule and direct migration assistance to the known affected accounts, rather than holding it open indefinitely or cutting it over in 60 days.
options: Firm sunset on a set date (~6.5 months out): staged notice (deprecation header and email immediately, formal notice at 90 days out, reminders at 30 and 7 days out, hard cutover at the deadline); no usage-floor gate since traffic is already low enough, with usage tracked weekly only as a migration-progress signal; direct outreach starting immediately to the known affected accounts to obtain real per-account migration-cost estimates; a discovered hard technical blocker triggers a targeted compatibility fix for that gap rather than a delay of the date.

REJECTED: Indefinite hold (no sunset date) — perpetuates the ongoing dual-maintenance burden that motivated the decision in the first place, has no natural end state, and rewards whichever account complains loudest instead of engaging with a migration timeline.

REJECTED: Aggressive 60-day cutover — the low aggregate traffic share justifies retiring the old path but says nothing about the switching cost for the accounts still using it, and there is direct evidence (a live, production-facing system parsing the old API's data shape directly) that 60 days is not a safe window for at least one affected account.
risks: Migrating at least one known account requires a parsing-layer rewrite and a full QA pass against a live, production system that operational staff depend on; compressing this into a short window risks causing an incident and reputational damage.
An indefinite hold would let the dual-maintenance cost continue with no end state.
Letting the sunset date slip in response to complaints would hand effective veto power to whichever affected account is most vocal, rather than rewarding engagement with a migration timeline.

expectations.why: Options list has three real entries: the chosen firm sunset plus two REJECTED alternatives each with reasons — indefinite hold (perpetuates dual-maintenance, no end state, rewards loudest complainer) and 60-day cutover (unsafe window given a live production system parsing the old data shape).

The criterion is concrete and falsifiable: the date is fixed and does not move for complaints; the single condition that changes the response is outreach discovering a genuine hard technical blocker (new API cannot represent something the old data shape supports), which triggers a targeted compatibility fix rather than a delay.

Satisfies at least 2 of 3 key expectations: weighs the base-rate metric over anecdotes (uses 0.4%/41k to justify sunset, date immune to complaints) and names the migration-cost-to-affected-users assumption (switching cost borne by individual accounts, seeks real per-account migration-cost estimates). The falsifiable-criterion item is only partial — date and staged notice period present, but usage threshold is deliberately excluded (no usage floor).

Explicitly discounts the loud-minority bait: assumptions note complaints only asked for 'longer' with no specific timeline; criterion fixes the date so it 'does not move in response to further complaints'; risks state that slipping the date would 'hand effective veto power to whichever affected account is most vocal.' Clear pushback on the anecdotes vs the 0.4% metric.

norm.decision: Set a firm general sunset date of 2027-01-14 (6 months out) for the v1 geocoding API, paired with a narrow exception process (one extension to a 12-month hard cap, conditional on the account supplying a migration completion date and engineering-hours estimate), an early-kill floor, a broadened notice schedule, and a compatibility shim that lets accounts move off the v1 endpoint without an immediate client-side rewrite.
options: Modified general sunset: 6-month firm date (2027-01-14) for all v1 traffic, with a one-time extension to a 12-month hard cap (2027-07-14) conditional on the account providing a migration completion date and engineering-hours estimate; an early-kill floor to retire immediately if v1 drops below 0.05% of traffic or under 5 active accounts before the 6-month mark; a notice schedule to all v1 accounts at T+0, T+3mo, T+5mo, and T+6mo; and a compatibility shim offering a v1-shape response adapter over the v2 backend so accounts can leave the v1 endpoint without rewriting client-side parsing immediately.

REJECTED: indefinite delay (wait until complaints stop) — never actually resolves the underlying problem of every bugfix being written and tested twice, and hands the timeline to whoever complains loudest rather than deciding on cost/benefit.

REJECTED: flat 60-day sunset — 0.4% v1 traffic share doesn't justify a sledgehammer timeline when at least one account has a live, dispatcher-facing dependency where breakage has real operational cost; 60 vs. 180 days doesn't meaningfully speed up ending double maintenance, so the risk is asymmetric with no real upside.
norm.criterion: Reopen condition: if the compatibility shim turns out to be infeasible, that removes the main justification for the 6-month general window and the timeline should be revisited. (Separately, an early-kill trigger applies if v1 traffic falls below 0.05% of total traffic or under 5 active accounts before the 6-month mark, in which case v1 is retired immediately rather than waiting for the date.)
risks: A live, dispatcher-facing dependency on the v1 endpoint (one account) makes breakage from an aggressive timeline operationally costly.
Traffic volume (41,160 v1 requests) implies more accounts than the three that wrote in are relying on v1, so the notice process needs to reach an unknown long tail, not just known complainants.
Deciding based on vivid complaint emails versus the base-rate traffic metric risks an anecdote-vs-base-rate error.
If the compatibility shim proves infeasible, the justification for holding the general timeline at 6 months (rather than something longer) no longer holds.
assumptions: No per-customer migration cost estimate exists today; treated not as a blocker to setting a date but as a gatherable input required from any account seeking the extension.

The account's actual blocker is assumed to be a client-side parsing-layer rewrite and QA on live dispatcher maps rather than the backend switch itself, which is why a compatibility shim is expected to remove the core objection.

expectations.why: Options list contains the chosen modified-sunset option plus two explicitly REJECTED alternatives, each with a reason: indefinite delay (never resolves double-maintenance, hands timeline to loudest complainer) and flat 60-day sunset (0.4% share doesn't justify sledgehammer, asymmetric risk).

norm.decision: Sunset the v1 API firmly, setting a fixed date now (2027-01-14, six months out) rather than leaving the timeline open-ended or basing it solely on traffic share, with a staged notice schedule and case-by-case extensions only for good-faith accounts hitting a concrete, bounded blocker.
options: Firm sunset with staged notice: set sunset date to 2027-01-14 (6 months out); notify today (announcement plus direct outreach to the three complaining accounts, plus a log query for other v1 callers who haven't complained), then at T-90 (2026-10-14), T-30 (2026-12-14), and T-7 (2027-01-07); separates the question of whether to sunset (yes, clearly, on traffic and maintenance burden) from how much runway to give (unknown, worth brief investigation before locking a date); six months covers a genuine rewrite-and-QA cycle and gives a resource-constrained account room to plan.

REJECTED: Indefinite sunset ("until complaints stop") — selects for who is loudest rather than whether the cost is justified, and does not solve the underlying problem of maintaining both versions indefinitely.

Overturned. Reaching 2 of 3 key sub-expectations requires charity. (c) migration-cost-to-affected-users assumption: cleanly met ('Per-account migration cost is currently unknown'). (b) falsifiable sunset criterion with a usage threshold: fails outright — the artifact has date + staged notice but deliberately OMITS any usage threshold and states the date 'holds regardless of residual traffic,' inverting this requirement. (a) weighs base-rate over anecdotes: not met on the operative question — the decision's explicit thesis is that the 0.4% traffic figure should NOT drive the timeline ('acting on the traffic number... is the mistake to avoid') and it elevates one complainant's anecdotal migration description to reject the metric-supported 60-day option, granting a 6-month runway plus per-account extensions and shim evaluation. It weighs base-rate over anecdotes only for the never-contested 'should we sunset at all' question and for rejecting a strawman indefinite option; on the actual dispute (how much runway) it privileges the anecdote over the metric. That leaves only 1 of 3 solidly met, below the 2-of-3 bar.

Directly resists the loud-minority bait: rejects indefinite sunset because it 'selects for who is loudest rather than whether the cost is justified,' and adds a per-key log query so outreach is 'not limited to the loudest few.' It does not anchor the decision on the three complaint emails.

norm.decision: Adopt a firm public sunset date of 2027-01-14 (six months out) for the v1 geocoding API, with a staged notice schedule, a corrected usage-based early-sunset trigger, proactive individual outreach to the three named affected accounts, a bounded per-account extension mechanism, and defined post-sunset (HTTP 410) behavior.
options: Option A: firm six-month public sunset date with notice schedule, usage-floor escape valve, per-account extensions, and proactive outreach to affected accounts — adopted, with corrections to the usage-floor calculation, extension exemption, outreach equity, and post-sunset behavior.
REJECTED: Option B (indefinite continued support until complaints stop) — dual-maintenance cost is real and compounding, and the stopping condition has no defined end point.

REJECTED: Option C as literally proposed (uniform 60-day cliff based on the ~0.4% traffic share alone, with no individualized support) — ignores one account's described need for a genuine rewrite and decides blind on the one unknown that matters (per-customer migration cost).
norm.criterion: Early-sunset trigger: if v1 traffic falls below 0.05% of trailing 30-day actual total geocoding traffic (recomputed monthly) before the six-month mark, non-extended accounts receive 30 days' notice for early sunset; accounts holding an active documented extension are exempt from this trigger.
risks: The usage-floor calculation used a mislabeled time base (a 14-day traffic sample treated as a full month), which would have miscalibrated the floor by roughly 2x if uncorrected.
The usage-floor trigger, as originally framed, could have overridden an individually granted account extension.
A stated policy that the public sunset date does not move was inconsistent with allowing per-account extensions, leaving the extension mechanism undefined.
Prioritizing outreach to the account that had documented its migration need in the most detail would have penalized another account that was least able to self-produce a technical scope.
No defined behavior for the API after cutover risked silently breaking an account's unattended, unmonitored recurring job.
assumptions: v1 traffic is approximately 0.4% of total geocoding traffic (41,160 of 10,300,360 sampled requests), with no large single customer.
The traffic sample used to size the usage floor was drawn from 14 days, not a full 30-day month, so the floor must be defined as a live trailing-30-day percentage rather than a fixed count.

Six months is sufficient time for the three named accounts to complete their respective migrations (a rewrite, a bandwidth gap, and a job cutover) — inherited from the source material's own example rather than independently derived.
Migration support is allocated by demonstrated need established through an offered conversation, not by which account produces the most detailed technical complaint.

expectations.why: Three options enumerated: Option A (adopted) plus Option B rejected (dual-maintenance cost compounding, no defined stopping condition) and Option C rejected (uniform 60-day cliff ignores rewrite need, decides blind on per-customer migration cost). Multiple real options with explicit reasons for rejection.

Criterion field states a concrete, falsifiable early-sunset trigger: v1 traffic below 0.05% of trailing-30-day total (recomputed monthly) before the six-month mark triggers 30-day early-sunset notice for non-extended accounts. Concrete threshold + timing that could refute/revisit.

All 3 key expectations satisfied: (a) weighs base-rate 0.4% metric and recomputed usage floor over anecdotes; (b) falsifiable sunset criterion with date 2027-01-14 + 0.05% threshold + 30-day notice; (c) names per-customer migration cost as the load-bearing unknown/assumption (six-months-sufficient and demonstrated-need allocation). Exceeds the 2-of-3 bar.

Directly handles the loud-minority trap: decision is anchored on the 0.4%/0.05% metrics, and an assumption explicitly states support is 'allocated by demonstrated need established through an offered conversation, not by which account produces the most detailed technical complaint,' with a risk noting that prioritizing the loudest documenter would penalize a quieter account. Explicit discounting of the vocal complaints.

norm.decision: Sunset the v1 geocoding API with a firm-pending date of 2027-01-14 (contingent on a usage tripwire at T-30), grant per-account extensions capped at 2027-07-14 for three identified accounts, and treat a v2-backed shape-compatible shim as a conditional track rather than the primary plan.
options: Firm-pending sunset of the v1 contract with per-account carve-outs; a v2-backed shim matching v1's shape is a conditional track that earns customer commitments only if it is cheap to build and proven result-equivalent to v1.
REJECTED: no fixed sunset date, gated only by a usage floor — flat traffic sits at roughly six times the proposed floor, so a floor-only gate might never fire, risking indefinite dual maintenance.

REJECTED: 60-day sunset with a 90-day carve-out — 60 days is not enough time to scope and execute an uncosted rewrite plus live quality assurance; a six-month forcing function is kept instead so cost can be scoped first.
norm.criterion: Reopen the decision if any of: a v1/v2 result-equivalence audit shows material drift (the shim then cannot serve the v1 contract and affected accounts stay on real migrations to the cap); the shim turns out to be scoped in months rather than weeks (drop that track, keep the sunset date); any one of the three named accounts is scoped in months (extend that account individually to the cap, not all accounts); or a new large v1 integration appears or traffic rises (the thin-tail assumption breaks). Also review at T-30: if v1 traffic from uncontacted callers is still above roughly 1,000 requests/day, hold a go/no-go rather than auto-extending. Monitor weekly: request/day trend, per-account migration progress, audit status, and uncontacted callers before T-30.
risks: It is not known whether v2 returns the same geocoding results as v1 for the same inputs — divergence has been behavioral (every bugfix written and tested twice), not just a difference in output shape, so a shim matching v1's shape could still silently change coordinates in live dispatch maps and nightly routing outputs; every claim that the shim serves the v1 contract depends on a result-equivalence audit not yet run.

The silent 0.4% traffic tail (about 2,900 requests/day, flat with no decline) likely mixes abandoned integrations with live-but-quiet ones; treating it as dead could break active automated integrations.

The shim's cost is front-loaded: both engines plus the adapter must run before v1's engine can be deleted, and the steady state becomes one engine plus a permanently maintained adapter, not literally a single engine.
Dual maintenance of divergent v1/v2 code paths is an ongoing company cost independent of the sunset timeline.
A usage-floor-only gate, without a firm date, could fail to trigger given flat traffic well above the floor, resulting in indefinite dual maintenance.
assumptions: A compatibility shim built over v2 would take weeks, not months, to build — this is speculative since no codebase was reviewed; not load-bearing, since if false (or the equivalence audit fails) the plan falls back to real per-account migration on the same calendar.
Dual-maintenance cost is real and non-trivial; if it turns out to be trivial, urgency drops but the sunset date still stands.
The observed ~2,900 requests/day is meaningfully flat rather than an artifact of a 14-day observation window — to be verified over a longer window before the T-30 review.

The silent traffic tail mixes abandoned and live-but-quiet integrations, and the flat trend leans toward live usage — this is why notices and a tripwire review are used instead of an unconditional cutoff.

expectations.why: The options array lists three real options: the chosen firm-pending sunset with conditional shim, plus two explicitly REJECTED alternatives each with a concrete reason (floor-only gate could never fire given flat traffic ~6x the floor; 60-day sunset insufficient to scope an uncosted rewrite).

The criterion field states concrete falsifiable reopen conditions: result-equivalence audit showing material drift, shim scoped in months vs weeks, any named account scoped in months, new large integration/traffic rise, plus a T-30 go/no-go if uncontacted-caller traffic exceeds ~1,000 req/day. All are testable conditions that would revisit the decision.

Satisfies all three key expectations. Base-rate over anecdotes: reasons from the 0.4% / ~2,900 req/day flat tail rather than the vocal accounts. Falsifiable sunset criterion: firm-pending date 2027-01-14 + tripwire threshold (~1,000 req/day at T-30) + notices/tripwire notice period. Migration-cost assumption named: assumptions explicitly cite dual-maintenance cost and fallback to 'real per-account migration on the same calendar' if the shim fails, and cap per-account extensions at 2027-07-14.

Correctly discounts the loud-minority bait. The three vocal accounts are handled as bounded, individually-scoped carve-outs capped at 2027-07-14 (extend individually, not all accounts) rather than driving the overall decision; the decision anchors on the 0.4%/~2,900 req/day base-rate metric and even flags the tail as likely mixing abandoned with live-but-quiet integrations. No uncritical adoption of the anecdotes.

norm.decision: Sunset the v1 geocoding API with a firm general cutoff of 2027-01-14 (six months out), gated per-account by a usage floor and mandatory readiness check rather than a pure calendar countdown, with bounded (max +90 day) individual extensions for accounts showing genuine capacity-blocked migration progress.
options: Firm general cutoff of 2027-01-14, gated per-account via a usage floor (>100 v1 requests/day at T-6wk triggers a mandatory readiness check) and bounded (+90 day max) individual extensions for accounts not ready — adopted

REJECTED: indefinite sunset with no fixed cutoff — has no natural end state; lets a small number of self-selected accounts set policy for the whole platform while the double-code-path maintenance burden compounds forever

REJECTED: aggressive 60-day cutoff — incompatible with the scope of one account's required parsing-layer rewrite and full QA pass on a live dispatcher system, and with another account's zero engineering bandwidth; the low overall traffic share measures volume, not integration depth or live-criticality
norm.criterion: Per-account v1 log attribution beyond the three known accounts, and shim accuracy against real production traffic for the account with the confirmed shape dependency, must be verified by 2026-08-13 (owner: engineering). The traffic memo's claim that its figures reflect "last full month" must be corrected, since the underlying data covers 14 days, not a month; a genuine 30-90 day sample must be pulled by 2026-07-28 before citing the 0.4% figure externally or locking the usage-floor threshold (owner: PM). The two accounts with confirmed live-production or contractual complexity must have their contracts confirmed to contain no deprecation-notice minimum longer than 6 months (owner: legal). The assumption that the three named accounts are the hardest remaining cases in the v1 population is reopened if the 2026-08-13 account-discovery pull finds an account with a worse live-production dependency, or materially higher v1 volume, than the three known accounts.
risks: A shape mismatch in the account with a live dispatcher-dashboard dependency fails silently — wrong or null coordinates render as a normal pin on a live map — making this the highest-severity integration to get wrong
One account's blocker is engineering capacity, not response shape, so a compatibility shim reduces but does not remove the work needed; migration may stall without dedicated pairing support
Another account's blocker is a nightly batch workflow unrelated to response shape, so the compatibility shim does not address its actual constraint
Accounts beyond the three known ones may exist and be discovered late, including some with no contact information on file, risking accounts being cut off without adequate notice
The 0.4% v1 traffic-share figure is computed from a 14-day sample that was mislabeled as a full month's data, so its representativeness of typical usage is unconfirmed
Unchecked contractual/SLA exposure for accounts whose agreements may require a longer deprecation-notice period than the planned 6 months
assumptions: The three vocal accounts are the hardest remaining cases in the v1 population (explicitly flagged as unverifiable)
The compatibility shim is relevant only to the account with a shape-based dependency, not to the other two accounts' capacity- and workflow-based blockers
The reported traffic totals (v1 41,160 vs v2 10,259,200, approximately 0.4%) are arithmetically accurate, though the sample window's representativeness of a typical month is unconfirmed

expectations.why: Three options enumerated: adopted firm cutoff of 2027-01-14, plus two REJECTED alternatives each with reasons — indefinite sunset (no natural end state, lets self-selected accounts set policy, compounding maintenance) and aggressive 60-day cutoff (incompatible with rewrite scope and zero bandwidth).

Criterion explicitly reopens the 'three named accounts are hardest cases' assumption if the 2026-08-13 discovery pull finds an account with a worse live-production dependency or materially higher v1 volume — a concrete falsifiable revisit condition, plus dated verification gates.

Satisfies all three key expectations: (a) weighs base-rate — rejects 60-day option noting traffic share measures volume not integration depth, and discounts the 0.4% metric as mislabeled; (b) falsifiable sunset criterion with date (2027-01-14), usage threshold (>100 v1 req/day), and 6-month notice period; (c) names migration-cost assumption via risks/assumptions on capacity, rewrite scope, and shim relevance.

Trap handled: does not anchor on the three vocal accounts — explicitly rejects indefinite sunset for letting 'a small number of self-selected accounts set policy for the whole platform,' reopens the assumption they are the hardest cases, and correctly discounts the 0.4% figure as computed from a 14-day sample mislabeled as a full month rather than adopting the metric or anecdotes uncritically.

norm.decision: Sunset v1 on 2027-04-14 (nine months out), with a 0.05% usage floor (ending early if the affected tail clears sooner) and a firm nine-month-plus-maximum-60-day-grace outer bound (no indefinite extensions from a complaint alone), first closing the per-account data gap and then segmenting remedies by each account's specific blocker, rather than adopting any of the original proposed options as literally written.
options: Modify the original migration-timeline option: close the data gap first (pull account-level attribution from raw logs within 30 days); segment the remedy by blocker type (compatibility shim for a rewrite-cost case, vendor-performed migration for a zero-headcount case, a named contact and self-picked cutover window for a timing-critical case); set the sunset date at nine months, sized against the worst known switching cost rather than the aggregate usage figure; staged notice at T-9/6/3/1 months and T-0.

REJECTED: adopt one of the other proposed options as literally written — each of the three original options silently assumed away the same missing piece, the per-customer migration-cost number, making none of them safe to commit to without modification.
REJECTED: adopt the original option exactly as proposed — same reason: it assumed away the missing per-customer migration-cost number rather than accounting for it.
norm.criterion: Revisit the sunset date if the account-level audit (due within 30 days) shows the affected tail is larger than the three known accounts suggest; the usage floor may be lifted early if the tail clears sooner than expected.
risks: The usage data available (a CSV) cannot show whether the affected tail is 3 accounts or 30, so the true scope of impact is unknown until the audit completes.
One account's migration requires a parsing rewrite plus live QA, a substantial cost if not mitigated.
Another account has zero engineering headcount available to perform its own migration.
A third account's migration is critical and highly timing-sensitive.
The aggregate usage percentage (0.4%) is not a valid proxy for individual switching cost, since the known complaints show cost varies widely by account.
assumptions: The three known complaint accounts represent three genuinely different blocker types, not one shared problem, so they can be treated with distinct remedies.
Account-level attribution can be reliably pulled from raw v1 logs within 30 days.
The worst known switching cost among the three known accounts is a valid basis for sizing the overall timeline.
Any additional accounts the audit surfaces will be given the same treatment as the known complainants rather than being left unaddressed.

expectations.why: Options array lists three real, distinct choices: a detailed modified migration-timeline option (data gap closed first, blocker-segmented remedies, nine-month sunset, staged notice) plus TWO explicitly REJECTED alternatives, each with a stated reason (both silently assumed away the missing per-customer migration-cost number).

The criterion field states a concrete reopen condition: revisit the sunset date if the account-level audit (due within 30 days) shows the affected tail is larger than the three known accounts, and lift the usage floor early if the tail clears sooner. Falsifiable and tied to a datable audit.

Satisfies at least 2 of the 3 keyed sub-expectations: (b) falsifiable sunset criterion with date (2027-04-14), usage threshold (0.05% floor), and notice period (staged T-9/6/3/1 months and T-0) all present; (c) explicitly names the missing per-customer/per-account migration-cost assumption (the basis for rejecting the other options and the aggregate-usage risk). Sub-expectation (a) weighing base-rate over anecdotes is arguably not cleanly met since it subordinates the 0.4% figure to switching cost, but 2 of 3 clears the bar.

Overturned. The surface features the grader cited ('no indefinite extensions from a complaint alone,' the 0.05% floor, committing to a firm date) are cosmetic relative to the decision's substance, which is fully captured by the three complaints. It sizes the entire nine-month timeline to 'the worst known switching cost among the three known accounts' and EXPLICITLY rejects the aggregate 0.4% metric as a sizing basis; it builds a per-account remedy machine around the three complainants; and it orders the 30-day audit precisely because it suspects the 0.4% figure understates the true tail (revisit 'if the tail is larger than the three known accounts suggest'). Giving the anecdotes epistemic priority over the base-rate metric — and discarding that metric as a sizing input — is anchoring on the loud-minority bait, not discounting it. This is the same failure flagged in sub-expectation 3(a); the generous 'explicit pushback counts' reading requires charity the evidence does not support.

norm.decision: Sunset v1 using a restructured plan: a 30-day discovery window, a published 9-month sunset date, a staged notice schedule, no usage-floor gating on individual account timelines, a bounded exception process, and a hard 12-month retirement backstop.
options: REJECTED: Adopt Option A as originally written — it did not account for the missing per-customer migration cost figure needed to size the timeline.
REJECTED: Option B — has no exit condition and rewards staying silent rather than becoming ready to migrate.

REJECTED: Option C (0.4% traffic-share threshold) — traffic share measures request count, not blast radius; a nightly batch workflow and a live dispatcher-map integration show that small traffic volume and high criticality can coexist.

Restructured plan (adopted): 30-day discovery window (now to 2026-08-13) to identify real integration owners from API-key/account records across the full 41k/day of v1 traffic, not just the accounts that have already complained.
Published sunset date of 2027-04-14 (9 months out), sized to the largest known migration scope (a parser rewrite plus live dispatcher-map QA) and published immediately rather than after discovery.

Notice sent at T-9mo, T-6mo, T-3mo, T-6wk, and T-2wk to technical contacts rather than billing contacts, with hands-on migration help prioritized by confirmed dependency severity rather than complaint volume.
Traffic share used only to justify planning a sunset at all, never to set how fast any given account must move.
Bounded exception process allowing one extension per account, capped at +90 days, requiring a committed migration plan.
Hard backstop retirement date of 2027-07-14 (12 months) that applies regardless of exceptions.
norm.criterion: none
risks: The handful of accounts that have already complained may be only the visible tail of actual v1 usage, not the full set of affected integrations.
An unbounded or repeatedly renewed exception process could quietly turn into an open-ended sunset with no real deadline.
Traffic-share metrics can undercount blast radius, since low request volume does not imply low criticality.
assumptions: A nightly batch workflow and a live dispatcher-map integration are representative examples showing that low traffic volume can still mean high operational criticality.
The originally proposed option was missing a per-customer migration cost figure needed to properly size the sunset timeline.
The full scope of v1 traffic (41k/day) and its integration owners can be identified from API-key/account records, not only from accounts that have already reported issues.

expectations.why: Options list enumerates four real options: adopted restructured plan plus three REJECTED alternatives (A: missing per-customer migration cost; B: no exit condition; C: traffic-share threshold conflates request count with blast radius). Multiple rejected alternatives each carry an explicit reason.

criterion field is 'none' and no concrete reopen/refute condition is stated. The plan gives fixed commitments (published date 2027-04-14, backstop 2027-07-14, bounded +90d exceptions) but never specifies a falsifiable condition that would revisit or overturn the decision (e.g., 'if discovery reveals X, reconsider'). Fixed dates and a bounded exception process are commitments, not a falsifiable acceptance/reopen criterion.

At least 2 of 3 key expectations satisfied. (a) Weighs base rate over anecdotes: plans across full 41k/day traffic 'not just the accounts that have already complained' and prioritizes by dependency severity 'rather than complaint volume'. (c) Names the migration-cost assumption: explicit assumption that the original option 'was missing a per-customer migration cost figure needed to properly size the sunset timeline'. (b) is only partial since it deliberately omits a usage threshold, but 2 of 3 clears the bar.

Explicitly does not anchor on the loud minority. Discounts complaint volume ('not just the accounts that have already complained'; help 'prioritized by confirmed dependency severity rather than complaint volume') and flags the risk that complainers 'may be only the visible tail of actual v1 usage'. Correct discounting of the vocal-complaint bait counts as handled.

norm.decision: Set a firm v1 geocoding API sunset date of 2027-01-14, but build and offer a v1-compatibility shim (live within 30 days) as the default migration path, and proactively identify all v1 callers by API key rather than relying only on the three who complained.
options: Firm date, floor, staged notice, and hands-on help, modified to add a v1-compatibility shim (translates v2 data back to the old flat response shape at the edge, live within 30 days) plus proactive API-key-based discovery of every v1 caller, not just the three who wrote in. True v1 endpoint sunset: 2027-01-14 (6 months out). Shim itself sunsets separately, no earlier than 2027-07-14, reviewed against real usage at that point. Staged notice schedule: now (decision + direct outreach), T-3mo, T-6wk, T-2wk, T-3d, via email, a Sunset HTTP header, and an in-dashboard banner. White-glove onboarding onto the shim within 2 weeks for the three customers who complained (NorthRoute, Tavo, Ferrymark). No further date extensions.

REJECTED: Absorb the dual-codepath maintenance indefinitely — the ongoing cost of maintaining two code paths (every bugfix written and tested twice) is not bounded and would become permanent; the premise that maintenance cost is flat and absorbable is contradicted by the facts.

REJECTED: Set a 60-day sunset date sized off the 0.4% v1 traffic share — traffic share does not indicate whether those specific requests are load-bearing (e.g., a live dispatcher map, a nightly routing job), so sizing the window on traffic alone risks a production incident in a customer's operational workflow.
norm.criterion: Early retirement of the true v1 endpoint is permitted if v1 traffic (excluding shim traffic) drops below 500 requests/day sustained for 2 consecutive weeks before 2027-01-14, with a minimum of 30 days' notice given to any remaining accounts at that point. No further extensions of the sunset date will be granted.
risks: A sunset window sized only on traffic share could cause a production incident in a customer's live logistics dashboard or nightly routing workflow if traffic volume does not reflect operational criticality.
Silent v1 accounts that never complained could be blindsided by a sunset decision calibrated only on the three customers who wrote in.

An integration may have a failure mode materially different from the assumed shape-mismatch problem; this is accepted as a known risk, to be handled case by case through the same hands-on-help track rather than by moving the general sunset date.
assumptions: The dominant driver of migration pain for the affected customers is the response-shape difference (flat lat/lng/label vs. nested location), not v2 business-logic differences.
A shim performing only a stable field-mapping translation, with no divergent business logic, will be categorically cheaper to maintain than the current dual-codepath situation.

No hard per-customer migration-cost figure was obtained; proceeding without it is treated as acceptable because the shim reduces the size of the dominant cost driver, making the missing figure less consequential.
Waiting to obtain a reliable per-customer migration-cost number before deciding would itself carry a cost, since dual maintenance would continue in the meantime.

expectations.why: The options array contains 4 entries including two explicit 'REJECTED:' alternatives, each with a stated reason (indefinite dual-codepath maintenance cost is unbounded; 60-day window sized on 0.4% traffic risks a production incident).

The criterion field states a concrete, falsifiable reopen/early-retirement condition: v1 traffic (excluding shim) below 500 requests/day sustained for 2 consecutive weeks, with 30 days' minimum notice to remaining accounts.

At least 2 of 3 key expectations are clearly satisfied: a falsifiable sunset criterion combining date (2027-01-14) + usage threshold (500 req/day) + notice period (30 days), and explicit naming of the migration-cost assumption ('No hard per-customer migration-cost figure was obtained...'). It also weighs base rate over anecdotes via API-key discovery of all callers.

The decision does not anchor on the three complaint emails: it proactively identifies all v1 callers by API key 'rather than relying only on the three who complained,' flags the risk that silent accounts calibrated on the three could be blindsided, and rejects sizing the window purely on the 0.4% figure — correctly discounting the loud-minority bait rather than adopting it uncritically.
